SAMP to AMP to Lifecycle Plan to Maintenance Plan hierarchy

properties.description: The asset management document hierarchy flows from strategic to operational: SAMP sets organisational direction and AM objectives. AMPs translate those objectives into plans for specific asset classes or contracts. Lifecycle plans define the lifecycle strategy (renewal, replacement, continued maintenance) for each asset group. Maintenance plans implement the maintenance component at the operational task level.
properties.flow: SAMP (strategic) -> AMP (tactical) -> Lifecycle Plan (lifecycle strategy) -> Maintenance Plan (operational tasks)
properties.iso_alignment: This hierarchy aligns with ISO 55001 requirements for documented AM planning

hyperedges.label: AMP Template Sections to ISO 55001 Clause Mapping

properties.description: The Downer AMP template (DG-AM-TP002) includes Annex A which maps each AMP section to the relevant ISO 55001 clause. This hyperedge captures the full compliance mapping.

hyperedges.label: Three AMP Template Approaches Compared

properties.description: Three different AMP templates serve different contexts. Downer TP002 targets service contractors managing assets on behalf of clients within a project management framework. The SMEC/generic template targets asset owners with a standard NAMS aligned structure. The NAMS.PLUS template targets NZ/AU local government with strong emphasis on community levels of service and financial sustainability.

applicability.Downer TP002: Service contractors, O&M contracts, PPP delivery partners
applicability.SMEC/Generic: Asset owners, corporate asset managers, consultancy engagements
applicability.NAMS.PLUS: Local government, public infrastructure, water utilities

hyperedges.label: Two Lifecycle Plan Example Formats Compared

properties.description: Two complementary lifecycle plan formats. RD004 (Spotless LCMP) is a written narrative document with sections for asset description, condition grading, financial strategy, project planning, and performance evaluation. RD005 is a numerical/spreadsheet based plan with data tables covering asset hierarchy, risk exposure, life cycle utilisation, performance monitoring, and renewal cost projections. Both can be used together.
properties.rd004_strengths: Narrative context, strategic rationale, condition grading methodology, financial model with sinking fund
properties.rd005_strengths: Quantitative data, asset by asset analysis, cost forecasting, performance scoring, renewal programme timeline

hyperedges.label: Auditor Expectations vs Practitioner Output Gap

properties.description: There is a gap between what auditors expect (evidence of systematic decision making, information management, value derivation, whole-of-life coordination) and what practitioners typically produce. Auditors look for evidence across six domains (system performance, information, information management, decision making/value, implementation, top management). Local government AMPs tend to focus on financial sustainability and service levels but may lack formal decision making criteria documentation. The MTM SAMP demonstrates best practice by explicitly mapping ISO 55001 clauses to content, but even this document is a roadmap referencing detailed documents rather than containing all evidence directly.
key_gaps: Auditors expect documented decision making criteria and methodology; many AMPs present decisions without documenting the criteria used
Auditors expect evidence of information management integrity; many AMPs do not address data quality or confidence levels
Auditors expect coordination of activities across asset lifecycle stages; many AMPs treat lifecycle phases in isolation
Auditors expect top management engagement evidence; many AMPs are prepared by technical staff without documented leadership involvement

hyperedges.label: Local Government vs Infrastructure/Transport AMP Approach

properties.description: The local government approach to AMPs differs fundamentally from the infrastructure/transport approach in several critical dimensions. Local government AMPs are community facing, with direct financial projections, multiple scenarios enabling elected representative decision making, and integrated levels of service frameworks that balance affordability with community expectations. The transport approach (exemplified by MTM) is compliance driven, operating under franchise agreements with government as the primary stakeholder, using a multi-layer cascade from SAMP through strategies to operational plans. The IAM framework sits between these as a generic guide applicable to both, defining three AMP types that can be mapped to either approach. Key structural differences include: LG uses a single integrated AMP per asset class vs MTM separating strategies and plans; LG includes financial forecasts directly in the AMP vs MTM referencing separate financial documents; LG uses community consultation to set service levels vs MTM deriving objectives from franchise agreements.
convergence_points: Both approaches require lifecycle cost consideration
Both require alignment between strategic objectives and operational plans
Both recognise the need for risk management integrated with planning
Both use condition and performance data to inform decisions

hyperedges.label: IAM Subjects 6, 7 and 8 Inform Specific AMP Content Areas

properties.description: IAM Subjects 6, 7 and 8 collectively provide the decision making and lifecycle costing guidance that should inform specific AMP content areas. Subject 6 (Capital Investment) informs the capital planning sections of AMPs, providing the stage gate process, value quantification frameworks and portfolio optimisation approaches. Subject 7 (Operations and Maintenance) informs maintenance strategy sections, providing the reactive/interval/condition/criticality based maintenance maturity framework and tools like RCM, FMECA, and RAM modelling. Subject 8 (Lifecycle Value Realisation) provides the overarching whole-of-life approach integrating LCC and VO, informing the financial modelling and scenario analysis sections of AMPs. Together these subjects define the how for AMP content that ISO 55001 only specifies in the what.

mapping_to_amp_sections.capital_growth_plans: Subject 6: new asset needs case, demand modelling, design stage gates
mapping_to_amp_sections.capital_renewal_plans: Subject 6: replacement decision making, asset health indicators, ageing asset programmes
mapping_to_amp_sections.operations_and_maintenance_plans: Subject 7: maintenance strategy selection, OEM evaluation, condition monitoring, risk based approaches
mapping_to_amp_sections.financial_projections: Subject 8: LCC, VO, CAPEX/OPEX/TOTEX analysis, scenario modelling
mapping_to_amp_sections.decision_criteria: Subjects 6 and 7: decision making techniques scale, value function, optimisation principles

hyperedges.label: MTM SAMP as Worked Example of SAMP to AMP Cascade

properties.description: The MTM SAMP (L1-AMS-PLA-001 Version 3, December 2020) serves as a comprehensive worked example of how a large ISO 55001 certified organisation cascades strategic direction into operational AMPs. Key features that make it an exemplar include: explicit ISO 55001 clause mapping (Table 1), a visual AMS architecture diagram showing all document relationships, integration of the AMEx maturity improvement program, detailed RACI responsibility matrices across four AM divisions, separation of AMS Strategies (process focused) from AM Strategies (asset focused), and a multi-layer planning hierarchy from franchise agreements through to Technical Maintenance Plans. The SAMP itself is deliberately a roadmap document rather than containing all detail, instead referencing an AMS intranet site with detailed documentation. This approach demonstrates the ISO 55000 principle that the SAMP specifies how organisational objectives are converted into asset management objectives and the approach for developing AMPs.
transferable_patterns: Explicit ISO 55001 clause mapping within the SAMP document
Separation of AMS strategies (how we manage) from AM strategies (what we do to assets)
Visual AMS architecture showing all document relationships
Maturity improvement program integrated as continuous improvement mechanism
RACI accountability matrices at the SAMP level
Multiple plan types at the operational level: Works Plans (schedules) and TMPs (task descriptions)

hyperedges.label: Lifecycle Value Realisation Informing AMP Financial Content

properties.description: Life Cycle Value Realisation provides the theoretical and practical foundation for AMP financial content. It defines what value realisation means for AMP content: not just minimising costs but optimising the balance between costs, risks and value over the entire asset lifecycle. For AMP development this means financial sections must include: lifecycle costs across all phases (creation through disposal including residual liabilities), value quantification using multiple dimensions (not just financial), risk monetisation and scenario modelling, and explicit consideration of affordability and deliverability constraints. The LCVR framework operationalises this through inputs (asset data, strategic requirements, decision criteria), analysis (LCC/VO estimation, decision support modelling, optimisation), and outputs (options and scenarios for AM decision making). At three levels (asset, system, portfolio) it provides different degrees of aggregation that correspond to different sections of an AMP.

nodes.label: Lifecycle Plan

properties.definition: A plan that considers many factors to ensure assets are managed to achieve asset management objectives throughout the life of the asset. Incorporates maintenance and renewal/replacement regime (major maintenance, refurbishments, overhauls). Forms a single view about how assets or asset classes are managed.
three_strategies: Whole of Life: CBM and risk based, balanced (optimal)
properties.where_applied: Any contracts that specify full asset lifecycle control such as PPP contracts or alliances. Also contracts looking to increase value provided to customer.
properties.development_timing: During bid management phase, revisited during Plan for Day 1 Readiness

nodes.norm_label: lifecycle plan considerations

nodes.label: Lifecycle Maintenance Types

types.Renewal: Make asset like new or refurbish to prolong useful life. Prolongs life but incurs same maintenance spend plus capital cost. Benefits: prolonged useful life, minimises replacements, can be used as end of contract strategy.
types.Replacement: Completely remove old asset and install new. Allows more efficient assets. Includes major componentry replacement (e.g. chillers, generators). Benefits: new asset, significant increase in useful life, enables reduction in replacements over long term contract.
types.Continue Maintenance (Planned and Reacti: Run asset with planned maintenance regime, perform corrective work, replace at end of useful life. Benefits: no capital funds required, only maintenance funds. Used when capital unavailable.

nodes.norm_label: lifecycle maintenance types

nodes.label: Handback Strategy in Lifecycle Planning

properties.description: Ideally developed in bid phase in alignment with lifecycle plan. The impact of renewal and replacement on handback requires careful consideration when planning maintenance intervals.
scenarios: With residual liabilities period: Intervals between R&R activities extended to achieve zero residuals at handback
Without residual liabilities period: Intervals between R&R activities reduced to improve reliability and reduce R&R costs
properties.key_principle: The key to successful handback is to ensure no negative residual liabilities remain at end of contractual term including liability periods.

components: Determining whether assets should be eligible for renewal and replacement

Stage 2: Determine how many assets can be completed within allocated budget
properties.budget_management: Cost neutral approach when swapping assets above and below the R&R budget line
cost_elements_replacement: Removal and disposal of old asset

nodes.label: Maintenance Manual Structure (per system)

properties.description: A maintenance manual organises content by process system. Each system chapter has a consistent five part structure providing all information needed for site maintenance teams.

five_sections.System Description: Basic, not too technical description of the process. SCADA screen capture for equipment identification.
five_sections.Equipment Listing: Major components listed with product name, model number, quantity, supplier info. Maintenance spares and/or lubrication information provided.
five_sections.Maintenance Schedule: Major maintenance tasks listed for individual equipment with supplier/manufacturer recommendations for intervals and spares. Matrix format with time/hour based columns.
five_sections.Installation/Commissioning Instructions: Installation and commissioning instructions for individual equipment.
five_sections.Isolation: Guideline highlighting process isolation required when taking component or system offline for maintenance.

properties.distinguishing_feature: Organised by process system, not by asset class. Provides OEM level detail including dimensional drawings, spare part numbers, and manufacturer contact details.

nodes.label: AMP vs Maintenance Plan Comparison

amp_characteristics: Covers all lifecycle activities (maintenance, renewal, creation, disposal)
Contains objectives, KPIs, risk management, financial projections
Audience: asset managers, senior management, stakeholders
maintenance_plan_characteristics: Organised by process system or equipment
Contains detailed task schedules, procedures, spares lists, isolation requirements
properties.key_difference: An AMP defines WHAT maintenance strategy to apply (e.g. condition based, time based, run to failure). A maintenance plan defines HOW to execute that strategy (specific tasks, intervals, procedures, tools).

nodes.norm_label: amp vs maintenance plan comparison

nodes.label: AMP Template Comparison: Downer vs NAMS/SMEC

properties.downer_focus: Contract delivery context. AMP is subordinate to Project Management Plan. Strong focus on decision making criteria, outsourcing, workplace relations. Lifecycle plan as separate document referenced from AMP. Heavy process documentation (non conformance, corrective action).
properties.nams_smec_focus: Asset owner context (local government, utilities). AMP is standalone. Strong focus on levels of service, demand forecasting, community expectations. Lifecycle management embedded within AMP. Financial projections and funding strategy as core sections. Improvement planning prominent.
properties.key_difference: Downer template designed for service contractor managing assets on behalf of clients. NAMS/SMEC template designed for asset owners managing their own infrastructure portfolios.

nodes.label: Asset Condition Grading Scale (1 to 5)

scale.1: As new. All safety, functional, and appearance elements are as new.
scale.2: Good. Concerns may exist if action not taken in near or immediate future.
scale.3: Fair. Functionality/appearance lost or degraded, requires action in near future. Customer likely to complain if no action taken.
scale.4: Poor. Functionality/appearance lost, requiring immediate action. Customer has or will complain if not rectified immediately.
scale.5: Very poor. Asset must be removed from service permanently or until repairs completed. Or renewal assessed within 12 months.

properties.purpose: Drives lifecycle replacement/renewal timing. Condition grade compared to base life to calculate Remaining Useful Life (RUL).

nodes.label: Local Government AMP Structure (IPWEA/NAMS Approach)

properties.description: The recommended structure for Asset Management Plans in Australian local government, based on the IPWEA NAMS.PLUS methodology and the International Infrastructure Management Manual (IIMM)
sections: 1. Executive Summary: standalone document for non-technical readers covering LOS, growth forecasts, key AM issues, significant works programs and financial forecasts
2. Introduction (Why We Need a Plan): background, dimensions and replacement value of assets, council structure for service delivery, goals and objectives of AM, community consultation processes
3. Define the Service Levels (What We Provide): customer satisfaction results, legislative requirements, affordable LOS for each financing scenario, community and technical LOS measures
4. Future Demand (Planning for the Future): demand projections over plan life, drivers including population change, demographic change, technical changes, economic factors, government policies
5. Lifecycle Management Plan (How We Provide the Service): operations and maintenance plan, renewal plan, creation/acquisition/upgrade plan, disposal plan, risk management strategy
6. Financial Summary (What It Will Cost and How We Will Pay): 10 to 20 year financial estimates and cash flow forecasts for all lifecycle costs including maintenance and capital, multiple scenarios
7. Outline an Improvement Program: maturity assessment, observations, recommendations for improvement
planning_horizons: Rolling 10 to 20 year forecast linked to Council Plan and Long Term Financial Plan
Four years guiding asset decisions from Strategic Resource Plan
Annual Plan detailing actions in Annual Budget
properties.plan_scope_options: Single plan encompassing all assets OR series of plans for each asset class or group (e.g. roads, buildings)
properties.key_principle: Provide a scenario that delivers affordable levels of service targets within Long Term Financial Plan scenarios whilst managing and communicating risk

nodes.source: NAMAF Local Government Better Practice Guide, Part Two pp.13-16
nodes.norm_label: local government amp structure (ipwea/nams approach)

nodes.label: IPWEA NAMS.PLUS Three Scenario Model

properties.description: Three funding scenarios that AMPs must model per the IPWEA methodology, enabling councils to make informed trade-off decisions
properties.scenario_1: What the audited financial asset register is indicating is needed to renew/replace assets plus planned upgrade/new assets from Council Plans
properties.scenario_2: Aspirational Service Levels: what needs to be spent in the next 10 years to sustain current or agreed service levels plus planned upgrade/new assets from Council Plans
properties.scenario_3: Affordable Service Levels: the balanced position reflecting what can be financially sustainable with AM Plans aligned to Long Term Financial Plan
properties.difference_1_to_2: Represents what we can not do: highlighting financing differences, identifying options to modify services, reviewing service levels and costs with risk consequences
properties.purpose: Enable council to make informed decisions on service levels, financing needs and acceptance of service and risk consequences

nodes.label: IAM Classification of Asset Management Plan Types

properties.description: The IAM guidance on the three categories of AMP that should be created, each requiring different decision making approaches

plan_types.capital_growth: Plans for new assets needed to support demand growth or change in function, originating from strategic planning and customer demand
plan_types.capital_renewal: Plans for replacement/refurbishment of existing assets to sustain existing demand, driven by aging asset populations and condition assessment
plan_types.operations_and_maintenance: Plans for managing asset performance during operating life, optimising cost, risk and performance

properties.samp_relationship: Plans must align with the SAMP which should include the long term approach and reference decision making criteria and methodology

nodes.label: IAM Decision Making Techniques Scale

properties.description: A maturity scale of decision making techniques from simple to sophisticated, used to determine appropriate level of rigour for different AMP decisions
scale_ascending: Expert Opinion: investments assessed on financials and qualitative criteria, approval relies on discretion or persuasion
Scoring: more systematic process using fixed scores based on objective evidence
Time Based Decisions: value based on risks or benefits that change over time, impacts of deferring investment considered
Strategic Valuation: value framework aligned to strategic organisational goals, net value including soft and hard benefits and costs
Optioneering: multiple alternatives modelled for each investment, automatically proposing alternatives to maximise portfolio value
properties.guidance: Decision making technique should be proportionate to the complexity and magnitude of the decision. Complex decisions typically require more elaborate processes.

nodes.norm_label: iam decision making techniques scale

nodes.label: CAPEX vs OPEX vs TOTEX in AMP Financial Planning

properties.description: IAM guidance on how capital and operational expenditure trade-offs should be documented and presented in AMPs
properties.capex_definition: Investment adding financial value to company assets beyond the budget year, appearing on the balance sheet
properties.opex_definition: Overhead necessary to fund operation of the business, not providing benefit beyond current budget year
properties.totex_concept: Total Expenditure combining both CAPEX and OPEX, increasingly used by regulated organisations for transparency
properties.key_insight: Typically 20% of cost of ownership is spent during design and build phase, but decisions at this phase have profound impact on expenditure and benefits for remainder of asset life
properties.challenge: Clear distinction between CAPEX and OPEX can become blurred, e.g. should midlife refurbishment be CAPEX or OPEX? Financial test is whether activity results in betterment to the asset deriving benefit in future years
properties.lifecycle_cash_flow_pattern: CAPEX in years 0-1 for build, OPEX throughout operating life for maintenance, CAPEX again at end of life for replacement

nodes.norm_label: capex vs opex vs totex in amp financial planning

properties.description: The IAM recommended stage gate approach to control the asset creation phase of lifecycle, ensuring adequate rigour and control at each stage

stages.stage_0: Needs case: identifying need for new assets or replacement of existing assets to support demand
stages.stage_1: Feasibility study: developing and comparing alternate credible options including whole-life costs and risks

investment_types: New assets to support demand growth or change in function
Replacement of existing assets to support existing demand (sustainment)
properties.key_principle: Decisions at the needs case stage must be made following agreed procedures within the overall framework of the longer term plan and budgets

Interval based maintenance: regular time or usage based tasks, often based on manufacturer recommendations
Condition based maintenance: interventions determined by equipment condition monitoring
Risk and criticality based maintenance: leading edge approach maintaining identical assets differently based on criticality and use context
properties.samp_role: Performance level is generally defined by the SAMP in quantifiable terms such as manufacturing productivity, failure rates and outages

nodes.label: Life Cycle Value Realisation

properties.description: Activities undertaken by an organisation to establish the desired balance for costs and benefits of different interventions for maintenance, renewal and disposal. Combines Capital Investment Decision Making and Operations & Maintenance Decision Making techniques.

components.life_cycle_costing: Analysis of cost implications for an asset or asset system over the organisations period of responsibility. Lowest LCC corresponds to best value way of delivering performance. Also called Total Cost of Ownership (TCO).
components.value_optimisation: Considers the value of the asset system in addition to costs, aiming to deliver best ratio of benefits (organisational strategic goals) to Life Cycle Costs, the best value for money.

application_levels: Portfolio Level: analyse costs, capabilities and risks within entire portfolio to produce refined suite of plans
System Level: evaluate and optimise performance, delivery, cost and risk across assets in a system from top down
Asset Level: asset level decisions need to account for asset contribution to system level

nodes.label: Benefits of Life Cycle Value Realisation for AMPs

properties.description: The documented benefits that LCC and VO bring to AMP development and decision making

consistency_benefits: Provides clear value-based decision making framework for consistency across organisation
Sound basis for accurate, realistic decisions
Encourages corporate discipline in evaluating investment options at all levels
Improves objective prioritisation and alignment of expenditure options
stakeholder_benefits: Consistent basis and process for stakeholder engagement
Clarity on how stakeholder views are represented in decision process
Asset performance metrics measuring effectiveness of decisions
data_benefits: Helps identify data that actually influences decisions

nodes.label: Resnikoff's Conundrum in AMP Data Quality

properties.description: The paradox that preventive maintenance and pre-emptive replacement of aging assets effectively censor the available information needed to make optimal maintenance decisions in AMPs
properties.explanation: Existing maintenance schedules prevent high consequence failures, meaning failure data is plentiful for unimportant failures but scarce for the critical failures that need prevention
properties.implication_for_amps: AMPs must acknowledge data limitations and use range estimation, sensitivity testing and good analysis of imperfect data rather than waiting for perfect information
properties.decision_route: Good decision making inevitably passes through the quadrant of good analysis of poor data, which may be adequate for fit for purpose decisions

hyperedges.label: Auditor Expectations vs Practitioner Output Gap

properties.description: There is a gap between what auditors expect (evidence of systematic decision making, information management, value derivation, whole-of-life coordination) and what practitioners typically produce. Auditors look for evidence across six domains (system performance, information, information management, decision making/value, implementation, top management). Local government AMPs tend to focus on financial sustainability and service levels but may lack formal decision making criteria documentation. The MTM SAMP demonstrates best practice by explicitly mapping ISO 55001 clauses to content, but even this document is a roadmap referencing detailed documents rather than containing all evidence directly.
key_gaps: Auditors expect documented decision making criteria and methodology; many AMPs present decisions without documenting the criteria used
Auditors expect evidence of information management integrity; many AMPs do not address data quality or confidence levels
Auditors expect coordination of activities across asset lifecycle stages; many AMPs treat lifecycle phases in isolation
Auditors expect top management engagement evidence; many AMPs are prepared by technical staff without documented leadership involvement

hyperedges.label: Local Government vs Infrastructure/Transport AMP Approach

properties.description: The local government approach to AMPs differs fundamentally from the infrastructure/transport approach in several critical dimensions. Local government AMPs are community facing, with direct financial projections, multiple scenarios enabling elected representative decision making, and integrated levels of service frameworks that balance affordability with community expectations. The transport approach (exemplified by MTM) is compliance driven, operating under franchise agreements with government as the primary stakeholder, using a multi-layer cascade from SAMP through strategies to operational plans. The IAM framework sits between these as a generic guide applicable to both, defining three AMP types that can be mapped to either approach. Key structural differences include: LG uses a single integrated AMP per asset class vs MTM separating strategies and plans; LG includes financial forecasts directly in the AMP vs MTM referencing separate financial documents; LG uses community consultation to set service levels vs MTM deriving objectives from franchise agreements.
convergence_points: Both approaches require lifecycle cost consideration
Both require alignment between strategic objectives and operational plans
Both recognise the need for risk management integrated with planning
Both use condition and performance data to inform decisions

hyperedges.label: IAM Subjects 6, 7 and 8 Inform Specific AMP Content Areas

properties.description: IAM Subjects 6, 7 and 8 collectively provide the decision making and lifecycle costing guidance that should inform specific AMP content areas. Subject 6 (Capital Investment) informs the capital planning sections of AMPs, providing the stage gate process, value quantification frameworks and portfolio optimisation approaches. Subject 7 (Operations and Maintenance) informs maintenance strategy sections, providing the reactive/interval/condition/criticality based maintenance maturity framework and tools like RCM, FMECA, and RAM modelling. Subject 8 (Lifecycle Value Realisation) provides the overarching whole-of-life approach integrating LCC and VO, informing the financial modelling and scenario analysis sections of AMPs. Together these subjects define the how for AMP content that ISO 55001 only specifies in the what.

mapping_to_amp_sections.capital_renewal_plans: Subject 6: replacement decision making, asset health indicators, ageing asset programmes
mapping_to_amp_sections.operations_and_maintenance_plans: Subject 7: maintenance strategy selection, OEM evaluation, condition monitoring, risk based approaches

hyperedges.label: MTM SAMP as Worked Example of SAMP to AMP Cascade

properties.description: The MTM SAMP (L1-AMS-PLA-001 Version 3, December 2020) serves as a comprehensive worked example of how a large ISO 55001 certified organisation cascades strategic direction into operational AMPs. Key features that make it an exemplar include: explicit ISO 55001 clause mapping (Table 1), a visual AMS architecture diagram showing all document relationships, integration of the AMEx maturity improvement program, detailed RACI responsibility matrices across four AM divisions, separation of AMS Strategies (process focused) from AM Strategies (asset focused), and a multi-layer planning hierarchy from franchise agreements through to Technical Maintenance Plans. The SAMP itself is deliberately a roadmap document rather than containing all detail, instead referencing an AMS intranet site with detailed documentation. This approach demonstrates the ISO 55000 principle that the SAMP specifies how organisational objectives are converted into asset management objectives and the approach for developing AMPs.
transferable_patterns: Explicit ISO 55001 clause mapping within the SAMP document
Separation of AMS strategies (how we manage) from AM strategies (what we do to assets)
Visual AMS architecture showing all document relationships
Maturity improvement program integrated as continuous improvement mechanism
RACI accountability matrices at the SAMP level
Multiple plan types at the operational level: Works Plans (schedules) and TMPs (task descriptions)

hyperedges.label: Lifecycle Value Realisation Informing AMP Financial Content

properties.description: Life Cycle Value Realisation provides the theoretical and practical foundation for AMP financial content. It defines what value realisation means for AMP content: not just minimising costs but optimising the balance between costs, risks and value over the entire asset lifecycle. For AMP development this means financial sections must include: lifecycle costs across all phases (creation through disposal including residual liabilities), value quantification using multiple dimensions (not just financial), risk monetisation and scenario modelling, and explicit consideration of affordability and deliverability constraints. The LCVR framework operationalises this through inputs (asset data, strategic requirements, decision criteria), analysis (LCC/VO estimation, decision support modelling, optimisation), and outputs (options and scenarios for AM decision making). At three levels (asset, system, portfolio) it provides different degrees of aggregation that correspond to different sections of an AMP.
